data.title: Unit Corporation GAAP EPS of $5.15, revenue of $24.72M
data.description:
• Unit Corporation press release (UNTC): Q4 GAAP EPS of $5.15.
  • Revenue of $24.72M.

  • During the year ended December 31, 2025, the Company repurchased 4,500 shares under its share repurchase program at an average share price of $30.13 (unadjusted for dividends paid) for an aggregate purchase price of $0.1 million.

    As of December 31, 2025, we had repurchased a total of 2,574,246 shares of common stock since emergence from bankruptcy at an average share price of $32.16 (unadjusted for dividends paid) for an aggregate purchase cost of $82.8 million.

  • The Company announced on March 6, 2026 that a quarterly cash dividend of $1.25 per share had been declared for the first quarter of 2026, to be paid on March 27, 2026 to shareholders of record as of the close of business on March 17, 2026.

data.title: Mattel bets on IP, gaming and entertainment to drive next phase of growth
data.description:
NEW YORK – Mattel (MAT) is accelerating its transformation from a traditional toy maker into a broader intellectual property and entertainment company, betting that its portfolio of global brands can generate growth across toys, film, gaming and live experiences.

Speaking at the UBS Global Consumer and Retail Conference on Thursday, Mattel (MAT) Chairman and Chief Executive Ynon Kreiz said the company’s strategy centers on extracting more value from its intellectual property while expanding beyond physical toys.

“We introduced a new strategy to grow our IP-driven play and family entertainment business,” Kreiz said during the presentation. The plan focuses on managing both toys and entertainment holistically while capturing value from brand extensions beyond physical products.

Mattel (MAT) owns some of the most recognizable brands in children’s entertainment, including Barbie, Hot Wheels, Uno and American Girl. Kreiz said the company’s portfolio gives it a strong foundation as consumer brands become increasingly important across media and entertainment.

“These are vibrant, thriving cultural brands,” he said. “The journey we’ve been on is transforming Mattel (MAT) from a toy manufacturer into an IP-driven company.”

Strategy shifts toward IP-driven play and entertainment

Kreiz said Mattel (MAT)’s core toy business remains strong, supported by industry growth and continued consumer demand for play experiences.

The global toy industry has expanded in 23 of the past 25 years and has grown more than 3% annually over the last five years, he said. Parents continue to prioritize spending on children, and toys remain a strategic category for retailers because they drive store traffic and encourage additional spending.

Mattel (MAT) organizes its toy portfolio across six categories including dolls, vehicles, infant and preschool toys, action figures, building sets and games.

Innovation remains central to the company’s growth strategy, Kreiz said, along with opportunities in collectibles and direct-to-consumer products. The company’s Mattel Creations platform, which sells limited-edition products directly to fans, has been seeing double-digit growth.

Mattel (MAT) is also expanding partnerships with entertainment companies and intellectual property owners. Recent additions include licensing deals tied to DC characters such as Batman and Superman, as well as collaborations linked to Netflix and Paramount franchises.

Movies, games and partnerships set up 2026 inflection

Kreiz described 2026 as an “inflection year” for Mattel’s entertainment strategy.

Two major film releases are scheduled for next year: Masters of the Universe, which will debut in theaters, and Matchbox, which will launch on Apple TV+.

The films represent the next phase of Mattel’s push into entertainment following the success of the Barbie movie.

Kreiz said the company’s broader goal is to create a cycle in which entertainment success drives toy sales and vice versa.

“Success in toys drives success in entertainment, and success in entertainment drives even more success in toys,” he said.

Mattel (MAT) is also expanding into location-based entertainment. The company currently has seven permanent attractions in development including theme park concepts, water parks and other experiences tied to its brands.

Examples include Barbie cafés, Hot Wheels attractions and Uno-themed social clubs.

Gaming becomes a strategic priority

Digital gaming is emerging as a major growth opportunity.

Mattel (MAT) is expanding its self-publishing strategy in mobile gaming and recently moved to fully acquire Mattel163, a mobile gaming developer that previously operated as a joint venture.

The business, which generated more than $200 million in revenue, will now be fully integrated into Mattel’s gaming strategy.

The acquisition allows Mattel to coordinate game launches, cross-promote titles and better leverage its brand portfolio, Kreiz said.

“It’s about creative execution, user acquisition and monetization,” he said, describing the economics of self-published mobile games.

The company expects to have four global games in the market by 2027, including two launching in 2026 and at least two more in 2027.

Investments expected to pressure profit short term but boost growth in 2027

Mattel plans to invest roughly $150 million to expand gaming, digital marketing and other growth initiatives.

The spending will weigh on profitability in 2026 but is expected to generate returns beginning in 2027.

Much of the investment will support mobile gaming and performance marketing aimed at acquiring new users. Kreiz said the marketing spend is designed to deliver measurable returns because the company can track user acquisition and monetization directly.

Other investments include building-set expansion through the Mattel Brick Shop initiative and growing the trading card segment, which is one of the fastest-growing categories in toys.

Mattel (MAT) is also investing in new technologies including artificial intelligence and strengthening first-party data capabilities to improve direct engagement with consumers.

Brands, capital discipline underpin expansion plan

Mattel (MAT) expects revenue growth of 3% to 6% in 2026, driven by toy innovation, partnerships and early progress in gaming and entertainment.

Hot Wheels, Uno and Mattel Brick Shop are expected to remain key growth drivers.

Looking further ahead, Kreiz said 2027 should deliver faster revenue growth and double-digit operating income expansion as recent investments begin to pay off.

Entertainment revenue, which typically carries higher margins because it relies heavily on licensing and royalties, is expected to contribute meaningfully to profitability.

The company’s capital allocation priorities remain focused on organic investment, maintaining a strong balance sheet and selective acquisitions.

Mattel (MAT) has repurchased about $1.2 billion of shares over the past three years and plans to buy back an additional $1.5 billion over the next several years.

Kreiz said the company believes its shares remain undervalued relative to Mattel’s long-term potential.

“It's rare to have a company that owns such quality assets with built-in global fan bases,” he said. “The question now is not whether our brands can expand beyond toys, but how much further we can scale that opportunity.”

data.title: Stoneridge outlines $750M revenue target for 2027 as MirrorEye momentum accelerates post-divestiture
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Stoneridge, Inc. (SRI) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Jim Zizelman, President and CEO, opened by highlighting that Stoneridge's “focused growth strategy, continuous improvements on material and quality-related costs and rigorous structural cost control enabled us to successfully navigate another year marked by very challenging macroeconomic conditions.” He emphasized outperformance versus end markets, noting “continued momentum with MirrorEye, resulting in sales of over $110 million or approximately 70% growth compared to the prior year.” Additional business awards for Electronics and Stoneridge Brazil totaled approximately $830 million in estimated lifetime revenue, including “the largest business award in Stoneridge history for a global OEM MirrorEye program extension.”

  • Zizelman detailed the “completed the sale of our Control Devices segment for a base purchase price of $59 million,” describing it as an important milestone that allows Stoneridge to focus on high-growth, high-return businesses and reduce complexity. He also announced the appointment of Natalia Noblet as incoming CEO, effective April 1, stating, “Natalia Noblet is the right leader for this company.”

  • Natalia Noblet, President of Electronics Division and incoming CEO, stressed the company’s focus on a global footprint and advanced technology offerings, saying, “Our portfolio is focused on advanced technologies and electronic solutions, primarily serving the global commercial vehicle and off-highway end markets.” She outlined MirrorEye’s role as a growth engine and discussed opportunities in connectivity, vehicle intelligence, and electronic controls.

  • CFO Matthew Horvath reported, “For Electronics, full year sales of $551 million outperformed our weighted average OEM end markets by approximately 430 basis points... MirrorEye sales, which totaled $111 million in 2025, resulting in growth of $45 million or 69% compared to the prior year.”

  • Robert Hartman, Chief Accounting Officer and incoming Interim CFO, explained that the commercial vehicle end markets saw a “7% decline in our weighted average OEM end markets in 2025 compared to our initial expectations of approximately flat end market conditions,” but signaled stabilization and potential growth ahead.

Outlook

  • Stoneridge expects MirrorEye sales to grow by approximately $50 million to at least $160 million in 2026, representing about 45% growth compared to 2025, with roughly $140 million in OEM sales. Noblet projected, “we expect to continue to drive market outperformance of 2 to 3x over the long term.”

  • Revenue growth for 2026 is expected to be approximately 4.2%, “primarily driven by continued MirrorEye growth as our weighted average OEM end markets are assumed to be flat.”

  • The company is targeting EBITDA improvement in 2026 to a midpoint guidance of $22.5 million, with significant improvement anticipated in the second half of the year.

  • For the medium to long term, Noblet stated, “we currently estimate revenue of at least $750 million in 2027,” and projected revenue between $850 million to $1 billion by 2030, representing a 5-year CAGR of 6.8% to 10.3%. EBITDA is targeted to reach at least $44 million in 2027 and between $80 million to $120 million in 2030.

Financial Results

  • MirrorEye sales grew to $111 million in 2025, up 69% from the prior year. Electronics division sales reached $551 million. Full year adjusted EBITDA was $28.6 million or 3.3% of sales, reflecting a 60 basis point decline compared to the prior year. Material costs improved by 80 basis points, and quality-related costs improved by $6.6 million, contributing to operational performance.

  • Stoneridge Brazil sales grew by $15 million or 30%, with OEM sales setting a record at $26.7 million. Full year adjusted operating income in Brazil improved by $4.6 million or 660 basis points.

  • The sale of the Control Devices segment for $59 million will be used to pay down debt and reduce interest expense burden.

Q&A

  • Gary Prestopino, Barrington Research, asked about legacy warranty costs after the Control Devices sale. Robert Hartman responded, “Any warranty related to Control Devices was passed with the business to the new buyer.”

  • Prestopino inquired about the revenue mix across connectivity, vision and safety, and intelligence and electronic controls in Electronics and Brazil. Matthew Horvath clarified, “We don't break out specifically by product category... the connectivity business is certainly more global than the other businesses, but... OEM sales doubling in Brazil, we are seeing some increased penetration.” Zizelman added, “Brazil currently about 15% of the business. Electronics business globally is 85%.”

  • Prestopino questioned the impact of MirrorEye’s growth on other electronics categories. Noblet said, “the biggest downside is coming from... products that we are really linking with the vehicle production.” Zizelman noted commercial vehicle volumes hit “all-time record lows” in 2025 but pointed to “a nice uptick... in December.”

  • Prestopino asked about MirrorEye sales force experience with truck production picking up. Noblet responded, “we see first very positive signals... in North America, but also looking at Europe. We start seeing also first increases... primarily in the second half of the year.”

  • Prestopino sought clarity on the telematics business model. Noblet confirmed, “we are quite successful in the digital services. So this is indeed the recurring revenue and the business that is completely different... The strongest market here for us is Brazil at this point.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a neutral to slightly positive tone, focusing on segment performance, business mix, and future growth drivers. Their inquiries highlighted interest in operational challenges and segment-specific growth, with no signs of skepticism regarding the company’s strategic direction.

  • Management’s sentiment was optimistic and confident throughout, particularly about MirrorEye’s momentum, the divestiture’s impact, and the company’s ability to drive long-term value. Noblet’s remarks exhibited a forward-looking, execution-driven tone: “I am committed to executing on the long-term plan that Stoneridge has in place.”

  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone shifted from defensive cost management and macro headwinds to a more proactive, growth-oriented posture, supported by the Control Devices sale and leadership transition.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from conservative market caution and cost focus in Q3 to renewed optimism around MirrorEye and market stabilization in Q4.

  • Strategic focus moved from managing volatility and evaluating Control Devices alternatives to executing a simplified, growth-driven business model with the divestiture completed.

  • Analysts’ focus evolved from production headwinds and cost controls to the revenue mix, warranty liabilities, and expansion strategies.

  • Key metrics showed MirrorEye sales accelerated, while overall adjusted EBITDA declined; operational improvements mitigated the impact of volume declines. The previous quarter’s EBITDA guidance ($30 million–$32 million) was revised downward to a midpoint of $22.5 million for 2026.

  • Management displayed increased confidence in long-term targets and outperformance, while analysts’ tone remained pragmatic and fact-seeking.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited “significant end market headwinds,” incremental tariffs, and “incremental quality-related costs of approximately $3.3 million” in Q4 as ongoing challenges.

  • The company recognized timing lags in recovering tariff costs, legacy warranty issues, and potential geopolitical volatility as headwinds.

  • Analyst concerns included the risk of lingering warranty liabilities and the impact of macroeconomic conditions on non-MirrorEye product lines.

Final Takeaway

Stoneridge management highlighted a pivotal transition, with strong MirrorEye momentum, a substantial backlog of business awards, and a sharpened focus following the Control Devices divestiture. The company targets $750 million in revenue by 2027 and up to $1 billion by 2030, underpinned by technology leadership and global execution. Operational discipline, cost controls, and growth in advanced vision, connectivity, and intelligence platforms remain central to Stoneridge’s drive for sustained earnings expansion and long-term shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Sagtec Global to acquire 60% stake in Malaysian Restaurant Group for $3M
data.description:
• Sagtec Global (SAGT) signed a term sheet to acquire a 60% stake in Malaya Heritage.
  • Malaya Heritage owns CNS Sdn. Bhd. and SS31 Kitchen Sdn. Bhd., which operate four heritage dining outlets in Malaysia supported by a centralized production kitchen.
  • The proposed deal is valued at $3M, consisting of $1.8M in fixed-price share issuance and up to $1.2M earn-out tied to revenue and EBITDA milestones.
  • For the financial year ended June 30, 2025, the subsidiaries reported combined revenue of RM15.34M (~$3.9M).
  • Sagtec expects the investment and operational optimization to drive ~70% revenue growth in 2026.
  • The acquisition allows SAGT to deploy its POS and enterprise software directly into restaurant operations while gaining recurring operating income.

data.title: Saga Communications outlines digital strategy investment and projects mid-single-digit revenue growth in 2H 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Saga Communications (SGA) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Christopher Forgy emphasized the company’s ongoing transformational change and its focus on digital strategy, stating that Saga "began to diversify our top line mix of deliverables, including our e-commerce platform, which is up 16% year-over-year and has created $2.5 million in local direct revenue in our Saga markets in 2025." Forgy highlighted growth in digital initiatives, with hyperlocal online news sites growing 18% year-over-year and delivering over $2.5 million in revenue with a 31% margin, excluding sales commissions.
  • Forgy detailed performance in blended solutions, noting, "Search was up 59% year-over-year and generated $2.2 million and targeted display was up year-over-year, 44.8% and accounted for nearly $3.5 million. Online streaming was up 8.6% year-on-year in total."
  • He discussed capital allocation, including ongoing quarterly dividends, special and variable dividends, and a stock buyback plan funded by the sale of company tower sites. He stated, "This entire project and then some was accounted for selling 22 of our Saga's tower sites. This plan also allows Saga to provide additional research and development and the resources necessary to develop our own growing digital platform."
  • Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer Samuel D. Bush reported, "For the quarter ended December 31, 2025, net revenue decreased $2.7 million or 9.3% to $26.5 million compared to $29.2 million last year. A large part of the decline in the quarter was due to reduced political revenue."
  • Bush explained the impact of unusual items: "We recorded a noncash impairment charge of $20.4 million, which included a charge of $19.2 million, which represents all the remaining goodwill that was previously included on our balance sheet, along with a charge of $1.2 million representing a reduction in the value of our FCC licenses in one of our markets." He added, "We recognized a gain of $11.6 million" from the sale of telecommunications towers.

Outlook

  • Bush indicated, "The first quarter is currently pacing down mid-single digits with Interactive up 26.4%."
  • He stated, "Including political revenue, the second quarter is currently pacing down, and we expect to end up down mid-single digits. We are expecting return to revenue growth, including political in the second half of 2026 with revenue increasing in the range of mid-single digits."
  • The company announced plans to "add resources to build the digital infrastructure we need to process the interactive orders that the blended sales process is developing as well as to provide our local management teams...with sales managers as well as digital campaign managers."
  • Bush projected that "station operating expense will be flat for the year as compared to 2025 when not considering the digital initiative expenses and up 3% to 4% when including an estimate for the digital initiative."

Financial Results

  • Net revenue for Q4 2025 was reported as $26.5 million, a decrease of $2.7 million or 9.3% from the prior year, primarily due to a drop in political revenue.
  • For the full year, net revenue was $107.1 million, down $5.8 million or 5.1%, with almost half the decrease attributable to reduced political revenue.
  • A noncash impairment charge of $20.4 million led to an operating loss of $9.5 million for the quarter. Without this charge, operating income would have been $10.9 million.
  • Net loss for Q4 2025 was $6.9 million. Without the impairment, net income would have been $8.2 million or $1.27 per share.
  • Station operating expense for the year was $91.8 million, flat year-over-year, impacted by a $2.2 million music licensing settlement. Without this, expenses would have decreased 2% year-over-year.
  • The company closed on the sale of telecommunications towers, recognizing proceeds of $15.1 million and a gain of $11.6 million. Net cash proceeds were $9.8 million, with $400,000 held in escrow.
  • A quarterly dividend of $0.25 per share was paid, and 219,326 shares were repurchased for $2.5 million in 2025.
  • Cash and short-term investments stood at $31.8 million as of year-end 2025.

Q&A

  • Samuel D. Bush stated, "First, not today," indicating there were no analyst or shareholder questions during the call.

Sentiment Analysis

  • With no analyst questions, there was no direct expression of analyst sentiment in the Q&A.
  • Management’s tone in prepared remarks was confident and forward-looking, with Forgy referencing directives to "do it fast, do it with force and do it with purpose," and Bush emphasizing Saga’s "strong financial position to improve profitability as our digital initiative improves both local radio and interactive revenue."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management maintained an optimistic and proactive tone, with a continued focus on transformation and digital growth.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted to explicit projections of mid-single-digit revenue growth in the second half of 2026, while the previous quarter’s outlook was less specific about timing.
  • Strategic focus has intensified on digital infrastructure investment and hiring for campaign management.
  • The company reported larger noncash charges in Q4, impacting reported losses, whereas Q3 losses were attributed mainly to music licensing settlements.
  • Analysts were active in Q3 with questions about revenue pacing, national advertising, and capital allocation. In Q4, there were no analyst questions, removing a source of external scrutiny.
  • Management’s confidence in digital transformation and blended revenue strategies has remained strong, with an escalated commitment to digital growth and operational efficiency.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified ongoing declines in traditional broadcast revenue and noted that increases in interactive revenue have yet to fully offset this trend.
  • Bush flagged nonrecurring expenses: "A noncash impairment charge as well as the previously disclosed retroactive industry-wide rate settlement with 2 of the music licensing organizations" negatively impacted results.
  • The company expects digital infrastructure investments to be "initially more costly than the revenue it will bring in, but it is a necessary expenditure to be competitive."
  • There is continued uncertainty regarding political revenue and the pace of overall revenue recovery in early 2026.

Final Takeaway

Saga Communications’ leadership remains committed to accelerating its digital transformation with targeted investments in digital infrastructure and blended sales strategies. Despite significant noncash charges and a challenging advertising environment, the company projects a return to revenue growth in the second half of 2026 and continues to prioritize operational discipline, shareholder returns, and strategic capital allocation. Management’s outlook is firmly focused on building digital capabilities to drive long-term profitability and mitigate declines in traditional broadcast revenue.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Brazil suspends tax on diesel after Iran war spiked prices
data.description:
Brazil's government said Thursday it has scrapped federal taxes on diesel imports and sales in an attempt to ease the Iran war's effect on oil prices, a move that could affect state-run ‌oil firm Petrobras (PBR).

The government cut the ​PIS and Cofins federal taxes levied on diesel to zero and imposed a ⁠12% tax on crude oil exports, as well as a 50% levy on diesel shipments.

The government said the move will cut diesel prices at the pump by ⁠0.64 reais/liter (~$0.122) due to the tax cut and a direct subsidy program that will provide payments to diesel producers and importers.

While Petrobras (PBR) has not raised local fuel prices, Brazil ​is still partly reliant on imported diesel, and distributors have been reluctant to sell it at Petrobras' ​prices, concerned about a possible price hike in the near future, Reuters reported.

Brazil Finance Minister Fernando Haddad said the measures would not affect Petrobras' (PBR) own fuel-pricing policy.

data.title: Stryker extends losses amid ongoing impact from cyberattack
data.description:
Stryker (SYK) shares fell for the third consecutive session on Thursday as the U.S. MedTech firm said it has yet to fully recover from a cyberattack on its Microsoft (MSFT)-based systems, blamed on an Iran-linked activist group.

Shares of the surgical equipment maker closed ~4% lower on Wednesday after Handala, a pro-Palestinian hacking group linked to Iran, claimed responsibility for the attack, marking the first time a cybersecurity incident targeted a U.S. firm since the onset of Middle East tensions.

“The incident has caused, and is expected to continue to cause, disruptions and limitations of access to certain of the company's information systems and business applications supporting aspects of the company's operations and corporate functions,” Stryker (SYK) said in an SEC filing.

The company stated that it has yet to determine the scope and nature of the event, including its financial impact, as an investigation into the incident is underway.

On Thursday, Stryker (SYK) added that while it is working to restore its electronic ordering system, it continues to have access to orders placed before the event. “They will be shipped as soon as our system communications are restored. Any orders that have come in after the event are being examined,” the company noted.

SYK added that it believes no malware or ransomware is involved in the breach, which it said is limited to its internal Microsoft (MSFT) environment.

data.title: Jones Lang LaSalle trades higher on 2030 financial targets, raised stock buyback program
data.description:
Jones Lang LaSalle (JLL) was trading higher as the real estate services provider introduced long-term financial targets and increased the size of its share repurchase program.

Shares were 2.32% higher at $296.23 during Thursday noon trading.

The Chicago-based company announced the launch of Accelerate 2030, a multi-year strategy designed to advance its competitive position across core businesses and drive value creation.

As part of the strategy, JLL set a target of 8% annual revenue growth, 12% annual adjusted EBITDA growth, and 16% annual adjusted EPS growth.

The company increased the size of its stock buyback program to $3B, an additional $2.2B authorization over the prior program. The company plans to imminently launch a $200M accelerated share repurchase.

data.title: ProFrac targets $100M cost savings and launches Machina AI suite as capital discipline advances
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: ProFrac Holding Corp. (ACDC) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Executive Chairman Matthew Wilks highlighted that "our results in the fourth quarter improved from Q3 with total adjusted EBITDA increasing 49% on an improvement across our two largest segments, Stimulation Services and Proppant Production." He emphasized strong operational execution, the early benefits of cost and capital management initiatives, and the exceptional results from the Proppant Production segment, citing increased volumes and improved logistics efficiency.
  • Wilks noted the challenging backdrop for the completions industry in 2025, referencing "tariff-driven economic uncertainty and OPEC's decision to increase supply in early April" as drivers of commodity price volatility, but stressed that ProFrac’s vertical integration and asset management platform differentiated performance during difficult market conditions.
  • Wilks announced continued progress on a business optimization plan targeting annualized savings of approximately $100 million by the end of Q2 2026. He stated, "on capital expenditure efficiency, we have already achieved at a minimum, the midpoint of our targeted range and expect to be at the higher end of the $20 million to $30 million target."
  • The company introduced Machina, a new well optimization suite integrating real-time measurement, analytics, and AI engineering agents, described by Wilks as "a complete well optimization suite... that spans the entire completion life cycle."
  • CEO Ladd Wilks reported, "on fleet utilization and pricing, we maintained a consistent fleet count in the low 20s throughout the fourth quarter and into Q1. More importantly, we saw improved utilization and operational efficiency across our active fleets and pricing remained relatively stable quarter-over-quarter." He indicated that January weather disruptions had a $8 million to $12 million impact on adjusted EBITDA.
  • CFO Austin Harbour stated, "in the fourth quarter, revenues were $437 million compared with $403 million in the third quarter. We generated $61 million of adjusted EBITDA with an adjusted EBITDA margin of 14% compared with $41 million in the third quarter or 10% of revenue." Harbour also detailed free cash flow improvement and significant reduction in debt during the quarter.

Outlook

  • Management expects total capital expenditures in 2026, including Flotek spend, to be in the range of $155 million to $185 million, with CapEx excluding Flotek between $145 million and $175 million.
  • Ladd Wilks stated, "while we expect Q1 results will be softer than our strong fourth quarter performance, primarily due to the January disruption, the operational momentum we're experiencing positions us well heading into the second quarter."
  • Management reaffirmed its commitment to cost discipline and indicated that as conditions normalize, operational performance is expected to recover, particularly in the Proppant segment and Haynesville basin.

Financial Results

  • Revenues for the fourth quarter were $437 million, up from $403 million in Q3.
  • Adjusted EBITDA reached $61 million, a 49% increase sequentially.
  • Free cash flow was $14 million in Q4 versus negative $29 million in Q3.
  • Stimulation Services revenues were $384 million in the fourth quarter, with adjusted EBITDA of $33 million and an 8.7% margin.
  • Proppant Production revenues were $115 million in Q4, with adjusted EBITDA of $16 million and a 14% margin. Volumes reached over 2 million tonnes, and third-party sales accounted for 43% of segment volumes.
  • Manufacturing segment revenue was $43 million with $4 million adjusted EBITDA.
  • Cash and cash equivalents at year-end were $23 million, with total liquidity of $152 million. Principal debt stood at $1.05 billion, with $136 million of long-term debt repaid in 2025.

Q&A

  • John Daniel, Daniel Energy Partners, LLC: Asked about the rollout and customer education for Machina technology. Matthew Wilks explained that Machina is installed on every fleet, and the technology enables closing more perforations per well, which could translate to significant D&C cost recovery.
  • Daniel, Daniel Energy Partners, LLC: Inquired about measurable production uplift from Machina. Wilks replied, "it's too early to tell, and it's a slippery flip for us to get into promising well results or an increase in production."
  • Saurabh Pant, BofA Securities: Asked about market sentiment amid Middle East disruptions and the impact on U.S. activity. Wilks indicated increased customer interest and discussions, especially for dual fuel and electric fleets due to higher diesel prices.
  • Pant also questioned deleveraging opportunities. Harbour responded, "we'll continue to actively manage it, and we'll continue to focus on making sure that we've got liquidity not only to run the base business, but also as opportunistic investments come around."
  • Patrick Ouellette, Stifel: Asked about Q1 Stimulation Services run rate and weather impact. Wilks said, "our exit in Q1 is going to be in line to slightly better than what we saw in Q4... it did compress the balance of Q1 and has given us a really tight schedule to run and benefit from higher utilization."
  • Daniel Kutz, Morgan Stanley: Asked about sustainable activity levels and utilization of spare fleet capacity. Wilks responded that the company "will remain disciplined and keep our fleet count where it's at, unless we see a true call on assets and activity."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on technology rollout, cost recovery, market disruptions, and operational momentum, with a neutral to slightly positive tone as they sought clarity on specific impacts and management’s strategic discipline.
  • Management maintained a confident tone in prepared remarks, frequently referencing progress on cost initiatives and operational execution, while acknowledging near-term headwinds. During Q&A, responses remained measured, emphasizing discipline and caution amid macro uncertainty.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone shifted from defensive and focused on cost-cutting necessity, to more confident, highlighting operational momentum and technological advancements.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Management’s language shifted from a defensive stance on cost controls and capital raises in Q3 to a more proactive and confident tone in Q4, emphasizing the realization of cost savings, operational momentum, and the launch of Machina.
  • Key metrics showed improvement: revenues grew from $403 million to $437 million, adjusted EBITDA rose from $41 million to $61 million, and free cash flow improved from negative $29 million to positive $14 million.
  • Analysts in Q4 focused more on technology, operational recovery, and capital allocation, as opposed to previous concerns about spot pricing, fleet count, and overall market volatility.
  • Strategic focus evolved toward leveraging technology and cost savings for competitive advantage, rather than solely emphasizing resilience.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited ongoing market volatility, weather-related operational disruptions, and uncertainty stemming from global geopolitical events, particularly in the Middle East.
  • The company is addressing these risks through disciplined fleet management, targeted cost reductions, and capital expenditure efficiency.
  • Analysts queried the recoverability of weather-related EBITDA impacts and the sustainability of cost savings, with management indicating confidence in recoverability and permanence of cuts.

Final Takeaway

ProFrac closed 2025 with strong sequential improvements in margin and cash flow, driven by disciplined cost management and early benefits from an ambitious $100 million optimization plan. The company launched Machina, an integrated AI-driven optimization suite, aiming to further differentiate operations and drive performance across the completion lifecycle. Management highlighted operational momentum into 2026, while maintaining focus on capital discipline, liquidity, and technological innovation to navigate ongoing market uncertainty and position ProFrac for future growth.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Dow, LyondellBasell upgraded at Citi as energy shock reshapes chemicals outlook
data.description:
Rising geopolitical tensions in the Middle East and disruptions to global energy flows are reshaping the outlook for commodity chemicals, prompting analysts at Citi to upgrade several major U.S. producers.

In a March 12 research report, Citi analyst Patrick Cunningham raised ratings on Dow (DOW) and LyondellBasell (LYB) to Buy from a previous investment rating of Neutral, arguing that supply disruptions and higher oil prices could boost margins for North American chemical producers with access to low-cost natural gas feedstocks.

The report also reiterated Celanese (CE) as the firm’s top pick in the sector while lifting earnings estimates across several companies, including Westlake (WLK), Olin (OLN) and Eastman Chemical (EMN).

Middle East disruptions tighten supply and raise prices

The shift in outlook stems from a growing supply shock tied to the Iran conflict and disruptions affecting shipments and energy infrastructure across the Middle East.

According to the Citi report, the closure of key routes such as the Strait of Hormuz has pushed up energy prices and disrupted petrochemical production across Asia and Europe. That has forced shutdowns or reduced operating rates at a number of facilities, tightening supply across several chemical chains. 

Nearly 20% of global oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz, making the region critical to global energy markets. The resulting spike in oil and feedstock prices has increased costs for producers outside the United States, where natural gas prices have remained relatively stable due to strong domestic supply. 

The mismatch between oil and gas pricing is widening the cost advantage for North American producers that rely heavily on ethane feedstock derived from natural gas.

North American producers gain cost advantage

That feedstock advantage is expected to drive higher margins for U.S.-based chemical companies, particularly in the polyethylene and olefins markets.

Citi expects polyethylene prices to rise roughly 12 cents per pound in the first half of the year before gradually normalizing later in the cycle.

The analysts estimate the pricing increases could expand integrated margins by about 4 to 8 cents per pound for producers, particularly those with low-cost U.S. assets. 

At the same time, disruptions to methanol supply from the Middle East, where more than 15% of global capacity may be trapped by the conflict, could tighten markets for downstream chemicals such as acetyls and MTBE, further lifting spreads. 

Those dynamics led Citi to conclude that the market’s long-standing oversupply narrative for commodity chemicals may be shifting toward a tighter supply environment.

Citi raises earnings forecasts across the sector

Reflecting the improved outlook, Citi significantly raised its earnings estimates for several major chemical producers.

The firm now expects Dow’s (DOW) earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization to rise about 22% compared with prior estimates, while LyondellBasell’s (LYB) forecast climbs roughly 32% on stronger polyolefins margins and higher derivative spreads. 

Other revisions include a 12% increase in expected 2026 ebitda for Westlake (WLK), a 14% increase for Olin (OLN), and a 9% increase for Eastman Chemical (EMN), according to the report. 

Despite the improved outlook, Citi noted that the duration of the conflict remains a key uncertainty. Even if tensions ease quickly, the analysts believe logistics bottlenecks, supply disruptions and low inventories could keep chemical prices elevated for several quarters.

The firm’s base case assumes two to three quarters of market disruption before supply chains fully normalize.

In the longer term, the report suggests geopolitical risks could discourage future petrochemical investment in the Middle East, potentially increasing the strategic value of North American chemical assets.
